section.title: Godlessness in the Last Days
section.sectionRationale: Paul lifts Timothy's eyes to the whole present age between Christ's comings, the last days, and warns that they will be marked by terrible times. He paints a searching portrait of self-centered, God-denying people who keep a hollow shell of religion while denying its power. Such people prey on the vulnerable and oppose the truth as Jannes and Jambres opposed Moses, yet Paul assures Timothy their folly will finally be exposed and their progress halted.
section.thesis: The last days are marked by people who love self, money, and pleasure rather than God, keeping a form of godliness while denying its power; from such Timothy must turn away, confident that although they oppose the truth and prey on the weak, their folly, like that of Jannes and Jambres, will be plain to all and go no further.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the catalog of vices that will mark people in the last days, climaxing in a form of godliness that denies its power; the way such teachers worm into households and exploit the vulnerable; and the comparison to Jannes and Jambres, whose opposition to the truth is doomed to be exposed.

verses.text: But understand this: In the {{eschatos|last days}} terrible times will come.
For men will be {{philautos|lovers of themselves}}, lovers of money, boastful, arrogant, abusive, disobedient to their parents, ungrateful, unholy,
unloving, unforgiving, slanderous, without self-control, brutal, without love of good,
traitorous, reckless, conceited, lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God,
having a {{morphosis|form}} of godliness but denying its power. Turn away from such as these!
They are the kind who worm their way into households and captivate vulnerable women who are weighed down with sins and led astray by various passions,
who are always learning but never able to come to a knowledge of the truth.
Just as Jannes and Jambres opposed Moses, so also these men oppose the truth. They are depraved in mind and {{adokimos|disqualified}} from the faith.
But they will not advance much further. For just like Jannes and Jambres, their folly will be plain to everyone.

eschatos.gloss: Last, final. The last days in the New Testament are not only a brief span before the very end but the whole era inaugurated by Christ's first coming and closed by His return. Paul tells Timothy these days, already present, will bring seasons of moral difficulty, so the warning is not merely about the distant future but about the age the church now lives in.

philautos.gloss: Lover of self. It heads the whole vice list and sets its tone: disordered self-love is the root from which the other sins grow. The catalog is bracketed by love words gone wrong, lovers of self and money at the start, lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God at the end, exposing a heart whose loves are aimed everywhere but at God.

morphosis.gloss: An outward form, shape, or appearance. These people keep the external shell of religion, its language and routines, while denying its inner power to change the life. It is the most chilling item in the list: not open unbelief but a counterfeit godliness, a form without force, which is why Paul says turn away from such as these.

adokimos.gloss: Failing the test, disqualified, rejected as counterfeit. It is the exact opposite of dokimos, the approved workman of chapter 2. These opponents of the truth, tested and found false, are disqualified regarding the faith. Their corrupt minds cannot pass the assay, and their counterfeit religion will finally be shown for what it is.

basic.context: Paul warns Timothy that the whole present age, the last days, will have hard stretches marked by widespread godlessness. He lists a long chain of sins that begin with love of self and end with loving pleasure more than God, and he pinpoints the worst: people who keep the outward appearance of religion while denying its power. Such teachers sneak into homes and take advantage of the weak, but Paul promises their foolishness will be exposed.
basic.hermeneutics: The vice list describes the character of a self-centered age, and Paul draws a clear command from it: turn away from people who counterfeit faith. The comparison to Jannes and Jambres, figures from Jewish tradition about the magicians who opposed Moses, presses the point that opposition to God's truth always ends in exposure and defeat.
basic.originalLanguages: The eschatos, last days, are the whole era before Christ returns. The list starts with philautos, lover of self, and its climax is a morphosis, a mere form or shell, of godliness without its power. These opponents are adokimos, disqualified, having failed the test, the exact opposite of the approved workman of chapter 2.
basic.typology: Jannes and Jambres, who resisted Moses, are a type of all who oppose God's truth and are finally unmasked. Their brief success and certain exposure foreshadow the end of every counterfeit religion.
basic.christ: The truth these people oppose is the gospel of Christ, the power of God for salvation. Christ is the power behind true godliness that the counterfeits deny, and He is the coming Judge before whom every folly will finally be exposed.
basic.god: God is the standard by which these lives are measured and found wanting, the proper object of love that they refuse. He limits the reach of evil, allowing opponents to go only so far, and He guarantees that their folly will be made plain to everyone.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 1 traces the same downward spiral of exchanging God for self and creation. Second Peter 2 and Jude describe similar false teachers preying on the church. Exodus 7 records the magicians who opposed Moses and were finally outdone.
basic.oneStory: Ever since the fall, human hearts have turned their love inward and away from God, and the last days intensify that rebellion. Yet God has always limited and exposed those who oppose His truth, from Pharaoh's magicians onward, and He will do so fully when Christ, the truth they resist, appears as Judge.

context.literary: The movement opens with a solemn marker, understand this, and unleashes a rapid-fire list of about nineteen vices, a rhetorical technique common in ancient moral writing. The list is framed by misdirected loves and peaks in the phrase a form of godliness but denying its power, followed by a sharp command. Paul then narrows from the general portrait to specific predatory behavior, and closes with a historical comparison that carries a promise of exposure.
context.historical: The false teachers at Ephesus gained access to households, then a private sphere, and influenced women who were burdened by guilt and hungry to learn but were being led astray. Jannes and Jambres were the traditional names, known from Jewish writings, for the Egyptian magicians who withstood Moses before Pharaoh, a comparison every Scripture-taught reader would grasp.
context.canonical: This is one of the New Testament's fullest catalogs of the moral character of the last days, sitting alongside Romans 1 and the warnings of Second Peter and Jude. Its enduring value is diagnostic: it helps the church recognize a religion that keeps the shape of faith while denying its transforming power.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To alert Timothy to the moral character of the present age, to command him to turn away from those who counterfeit godliness, and to reassure him that such opponents of the truth, however active, are limited and will be exposed as fools.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vice list and the account of predatory teachers are descriptive, portraying a recurring reality of the last days. The clear prescription is the command turn away from such as these, and by implication to be the opposite: genuinely godly, submitted to the power of God.

debates.question: When are the last days that Paul describes?

views.label: The whole era between Christ's comings
views.case: The New Testament regularly calls the present age the last days, so Paul warns of difficulties already breaking in, not only a final future crisis.

views.label: Intensifying seasons, especially near the end
views.case: The phrase terrible times will come may point to particular periods of concentrated evil that recur and sharpen as history moves toward Christ's return.

debates.bearing: Either way the warning is urgent and present: the church in every generation must recognize and resist counterfeit godliness now.

debates.question: Why single out the vulnerable women in verse 6?

views.label: A description of the predators' tactics
views.case: The point is the deceivers' method, exploiting those weighed down by guilt and unsettled by passions, not a blanket statement about women.

views.label: A caution about untethered learning
views.case: Always learning but never arriving warns against a restless appetite for novelty that never submits to the truth, a danger for anyone.

debates.bearing: The verse exposes how false teachers prey on the guilt-burdened and unanchored, calling the church to protect the vulnerable and to ground all learning in the truth.

study.originalLanguages: The movement is shaped by pointed Greek terms. The eschatos, last days, name the whole present age inaugurated by Christ, so the warning is for now, not just later. The vice list is framed by disordered loves: it opens with philautos, lover of self, and closes with lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God, a heart whose affections point everywhere but heavenward. Its darkest note is morphosis, a mere form or outward shape, of godliness that denies the very power that makes it real, a counterfeit shell. And the opponents are adokimos, disqualified, having failed the test, the exact opposite of the dokimos, approved, workman of the previous movement. The contrast is deliberate: God approves the genuine and rejects the counterfeit.

typology.fulfillment: A type of all who resist God's truth, granted brief success but finally exposed and defeated, as every counterfeit religion will be.

typology.type: A form of godliness without power
typology.fulfillment: The counterfeit that awaits unmasking when Christ, the true power of godliness, appears as Judge.

study.christ: The truth these people oppose is the gospel of Jesus Christ, and the power they deny is His, the power of God that alone makes godliness real rather than a hollow shell. Christ is the answer to every misdirected love in the list, the One worthy of the love they lavish on self, money, and pleasure. And He is the coming Judge before whom the folly of all counterfeit religion will at last be plain to everyone, just as the magicians' trickery was finally outshone by the power of God through Moses.
study.god: God is the true object of love that this godless age refuses, the standard against which its self-worship is exposed. He is sovereign over the spread of evil, decreeing that the opponents will not advance much further. Though counterfeit faith may flourish for a season and prey on the weak, God guarantees its exposure, for He will not allow His truth to be finally overthrown, and He measures every heart by whether it is genuinely His or merely wears the form.

crossReferences.note: A parallel catalog of vices flowing from refusing to honor God, echoing this portrait of the last days.
False teachers who secretly bring in destructive heresies and exploit believers, like those described here.
The Egyptian magicians, traditionally named Jannes and Jambres, who opposed Moses and were finally overcome.
People who honor God with their lips while their hearts are far from Him, a form of godliness without its power.

study.oneStory: Since the fall the human heart has curved inward, loving self, money, and pleasure in place of God, and the last days bring that rebellion to a head. Yet from Pharaoh's magicians to the deceivers at Ephesus, God has always limited and unmasked those who resist His truth. The story runs toward the day when Christ, the truth they oppose and the power they deny, appears to expose every counterfeit and vindicate the genuine faith of His people.

sermon.title: Times of Difficulty
sermon.why: Part of Begg's series A Study in 2 Timothy, this message expounds 2 Timothy 3:1-5, the marks of the last days and the searching vice list that climaxes in a form of godliness that denies its power, calling the church to genuine, Spirit-empowered faith rather than a hollow shell.

quiz.q: What are the last days Paul refers to in verse 1?

options.text: The whole present age between Christ's first and second comings.
options.note: The New Testament calls this present era the last days, already marked by difficulty.

options.text: Only a brief final week just before the world ends.
options.note: The phrase reaches wider than a final week; it names the present age.

options.text: A period long past that has no bearing on us.
options.note: Paul warns of difficulties already breaking in and continuing now.

options.text: The era Christ inaugurated, in which seasons of difficulty come.
options.note: True and close, capturing that these difficult times fall within the present age.

quiz.q: What sits at the head of Paul's vice list, setting its tone?

options.text: Love of self, the root from which the other sins grow.
options.note: The list begins with lovers of self and is framed by disordered loves.

options.text: Physical violence above every other sin.
options.note: Brutality appears, but the list is headed by love of self, not violence.

options.text: Honest doubt about God's existence.
options.note: The problem is misdirected love and counterfeit religion, not honest doubt.

options.text: Misdirected love, aimed at self, money, and pleasure rather than God.
options.note: True and close, capturing the frame of loves gone wrong that brackets the list.

quiz.q: What is most chilling about the people Paul describes in verse 5?

options.text: They keep a form of godliness while denying its power.
options.note: The counterfeit shell of religion without transforming power is the sharpest warning.

options.text: They openly reject all religion and mock believers.
options.note: The danger here is subtler: they keep religion's form, not reject it openly.

options.text: They are sincere seekers who simply need more time.
options.note: Paul calls them to be turned away from, not patiently waited for.

options.text: They wear the outward shape of faith without its inner reality.
options.note: True and close, restating the form of godliness that denies its power.

quiz.q: How does Paul use the example of Jannes and Jambres?

options.text: To show that opposing God's truth ends in exposure and defeat.
options.note: Their folly, like the magicians who opposed Moses, will be plain to all.

options.text: To prove that false teachers always succeed in the end.
options.note: The opposite: Paul says they will not advance much further.

options.text: To celebrate their impressive magical powers.
options.note: They are cited as opponents doomed to exposure, not as admirable figures.

options.text: To assure Timothy that such opponents are limited by God.
options.note: True and close; their reach is limited and their folly finally revealed.

quiz.q: What does Paul tell Timothy to do about such people?

options.text: Turn away from them.
options.note: Verse 5 ends with the direct command, turn away from such as these.

options.text: Join their households to learn their methods.
options.note: It is these deceivers who worm into households; Timothy is to avoid them.

options.text: Debate them endlessly until they concede.
options.note: Paul commands turning away, not endless dispute with counterfeits.

options.text: Keep his distance and refuse their influence.
options.note: True and close, restating the command to turn away from such as these.

questions: Paul's vice list begins with love of self. Where do you see disordered self-love shaping your own desires and choices more than love for God?
A form of godliness without power is religion's most dangerous counterfeit. Are there areas where you are keeping the outward shape of faith while resisting its power to change you?
The deceivers preyed on people who were always learning but never arriving at the truth. How do you make sure your learning actually anchors you in the truth rather than just feeding restlessness?
Paul tells Timothy to turn away from certain influences. Are there voices or relationships you need to step back from for the health of your faith?
God promises that the folly of those who oppose the truth will finally be exposed. How does that assurance steady you when counterfeit faith seems to be winning?

translationNotes.note: The last days in the New Testament denote the whole era inaugurated by Christ's first coming, not only a final future crisis.
The Greek stacks roughly nineteen vices in a compact list; several are single words rendered by short English phrases, and the whole is framed by misdirected love.
Vulnerable women translates a term for weak or burdened little women; the emphasis falls on the predators' exploitation of the guilt-laden and unsettled.
Jannes and Jambres are the traditional Jewish names for the Egyptian magicians who opposed Moses; the BSB footnote cites Jasher 79:27 for the tradition.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Timothy 3:1-9. No em dashes present in this movement's text.
